<!-- Topic 6: Arrays with Three or More Dimensions -->
<!-- Total slides: 3 -->

# Arrays with Three or More Dimensions

---

## Does the pattern extend? {.smaller}

- Yes — each additional bracket adds one more level of nesting
- Three indices: layer, row, column

::: notes
Total slides: 3
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## Declaration, access, and traversal

```{.cpp}
int cube[2][3][4];          // 2 layers, 3 rows, 4 columns

cube[0][1][2] = 99;         // layer 0, row 1, column 2

for (int l = 0; l < 2; l++) {
    for (int r = 0; r < 3; r++) {
        for (int c = 0; c < 4; c++) {
            cube[l][r][c] = l * 12 + r * 4 + c;
        }
    }
}
```

- Each additional dimension requires one more nested loop
- The pattern is mechanical — the mental model is the hard part

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Summary

- Higher-dimensional arrays follow the same declaration and access pattern — add brackets, add loops
- In practice, 3D arrays are rare; most multi-dimensional needs are handled with 2D arrays or vectors of vectors

<!-- Slide 3 -->
